title: Safety Training

jokes.text: Drew. I'm Drew now. / Oh... Drew. / Sorry. / Apology not accepted.
jokes.setup: Andy announces his new name change, Pam apologizes for using the wrong name
jokes.punchline: Andy rejects the apology because it wasn't necessary
jokes.explanation: Strong character-driven humor showing Andy's post-anger management personality. The twist that he rejects an unnecessary apology is both unexpected and perfectly in character.

jokes.text: And... a bunch of new techniques for dealing with the grumpies.
jokes.setup: Andy describing his anger management transformation seriously
jokes.punchline: Calling anger 'the grumpies' in childish language
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Andy's overcorrection from anger management, using infantilizing language for serious emotions.

jokes.text: You can call me Drew. / No, I'm not gonna call you that. / Cool. I can't control what you do. I can only control what I do.
jokes.setup: Andy tries to enforce his name change using therapy speak
jokes.punchline: Jim flat out refuses and Andy responds with forced zen acceptance
jokes.explanation: Excellent character interplay. Jim's blunt refusal paired with Andy's therapeutic response creates perfect cringe comedy that's true to both characters.

jokes.text: I have decided to shun Andy Bernard for the next three years. Which I'm looking forward to. It's an Amish technique. It's like slapping someone with silence.
jokes.setup: Dwight announces he's shunning Andy
jokes.punchline: Describes it as 'slapping someone with silence' and that he's looking forward to it
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dwight moment. The phrase 'slapping someone with silence' is both poetic and absurd, and his enthusiasm for shunning is quintessential character comedy.

jokes.text: I was shunned from the age of four until my sixth birthday for not saving the excess oil from a can of tuna.
jokes.setup: Dwight explaining his experience being shunned
jokes.punchline: The absurdly specific and minor offense that got him shunned as a child
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character work. The specificity of 'excess oil from a can of tuna' and the fact that a child was shunned for two years over this creates layered humor that's both absurd and darkly funny.

jokes.text: Andy, Dwight says welcome back, and he could use a hug. / Okay, tell him that that's not true. / Dwight says that he actually doesn't know one single fact about bear attacks.
jokes.setup: Jim translating between Dwight and Andy during the shunning
jokes.punchline: Jim completely lies about what Dwight said, escalating to insulting Dwight's bear expertise
jokes.explanation: Classic Jim prank structure. He exploits the shunning situation to mess with both parties, hitting Dwight where it hurts (bear knowledge) while maintaining plausible deniability.

jokes.text: Tell him bears can climb faster than they can run. / Jim! / Andy... Nah, that's too far.
jokes.setup: Dwight desperate to correct the bear misinformation through Jim
jokes.punchline: Jim decides sharing actual bear facts is 'too far' compared to his lies
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation and character moment. Jim's moral line being drawn at sharing accurate bear facts (rather than his previous lies) is beautifully absurd.

jokes.text: And if I know Darryl, it gonna be zoppity.
jokes.setup: Michael building up Darryl's warehouse safety presentation
jokes.punchline: Uses the nonsense word 'zoppity' as if it's slang
jokes.explanation: Quintessential Michael trying to sound cool with made-up slang. 'Zoppity' is memorable and perfectly captures his character's linguistic pretensions.

jokes.text: I thought your name was Pudge. / No, it's always been Madge.
jokes.setup: Michael confidently calls warehouse worker Pudge
jokes.punchline: She corrects him - her name has always been Madge
jokes.explanation: Standard Michael incompetence joke. Shows his disconnection from warehouse workers but is fairly predictable for his character.

jokes.text: kicked the ladder out from under me and yelled... 'Hey, Darryl, how's it hangin'?'
jokes.setup: Darryl describing his accident caused by an office worker
jokes.punchline: The office worker made a pun about 'hanging' while Darryl was literally hanging/falling
jokes.explanation: Dark humor that works on multiple levels. The pun is terrible timing but the structure of the reveal makes it effectively cringe-worthy.

jokes.text: Bail 'er? I hardly know her.
jokes.setup: Darryl asking about people getting hurt by the bailer
jokes.punchline: Michael makes a 'I hardly know her' joke with 'bailer'
jokes.explanation: Classic terrible Michael pun at inappropriate time. While predictable, it's perfectly timed for maximum inappropriateness during safety discussion.

jokes.text: Ever since March Madness ended, I am so bored.
jokes.setup: Discussion of dangerous workplace accidents
jokes.punchline: Michael is bored since March Madness ended, implying he's treating safety like sports betting
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Michael's inappropriate priorities and need for entertainment, even from workplace safety statistics.

jokes.text: It's a big, red trash compactor. / It's not a trash compactor! It's a bailer.
jokes.setup: Michael dismissively describes the dangerous bailer
jokes.punchline: Darryl's exasperated correction emphasizing the difference
jokes.explanation: Standard Michael minimization paired with Darryl's justified frustration. Solid but unremarkable workplace comedy.

jokes.text: Toby now has the floor. And he is going to try not to screw this up. Like everything else in his life.
jokes.setup: Michael introducing Toby's safety presentation
jokes.punchline: Adds cruel personal insult about Toby screwing up everything in his life
jokes.explanation: Typical Michael cruelty to Toby. While in character, it's fairly standard for their dynamic without adding much originality.

jokes.text: Wow, that is... that time really adds up. That's like a half an hour every hour. / Take them at the same time.
jokes.setup: Michael calculating all the break time recommendations
jokes.punchline: Toby's practical suggestion to take all breaks simultaneously
jokes.explanation: Good observational humor about workplace safety recommendations. Toby's deadpan solution is practical and funny.

jokes.text: Like sometimes, computers can explode. / Can they not? / No, no.
jokes.setup: Michael suggests computers can explode as a safety hazard
jokes.punchline: Toby's confused response and deflation when it's not true
jokes.explanation: Michael's attempt to spice up safety training with false dangers, and Toby's brief hope that he might have something exciting to share.

jokes.text: What about a long-sleeved tee? / Well, that'll work. / Long johns? A shawl?
jokes.setup: Discussion of appropriate office warming clothes
jokes.punchline: Michael's increasingly absurd suggestions ending with 'shawl'
jokes.explanation: Simple escalation comedy with Michael pushing boundaries of office appropriate clothing. 'Shawl' is the unexpected peak.

jokes.text: Sedimentary. / Sedentary. / Yes, 'which can contribute to heart disease.' / Heart disease kills more people than bailers. / That's called having a fat butt, Michael.
jokes.setup: Toby mispronounces 'sedentary' as 'sedimentary', discussion of health risks
jokes.punchline: Darryl's blunt translation to 'fat butt disease'
jokes.explanation: Multi-layered: Toby's malapropism, Michael's defensive statistics, and Darryl's cutting simplification. Good escalation and character voices.

jokes.text: Excuse me, Sea Monster, you weigh, like, 1,000 pounds. / Yeah, I bet you'd like to swim with this sea monster, wouldn't you?
jokes.setup: Kelly insults warehouse worker by calling him 'Sea Monster'
jokes.punchline: Worker's flirtatious response turning the insult into innuendo
jokes.explanation: Unexpected turn where an insult becomes flirtation. The worker's confidence in turning 'Sea Monster' into something appealing is genuinely surprising.

jokes.text: You live a sweet little NERF-y life. Sittin' on your biscuit. Never havin' to risk it.
jokes.setup: Darryl dismissing the office workers' soft lifestyle
jokes.punchline: The rhythmic phrase 'sittin' on your biscuit, never havin' to risk it'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant rhythmic wordplay. 'Sittin' on your biscuit, never havin' to risk it' is pure poetry with perfect internal rhyme and captures the character dynamic beautifully.

jokes.text: I worked in a warehouse... Men's Wearhouse. I was a greeter.
jokes.setup: Michael claims warehouse experience to counter Darryl
jokes.punchline: Reveals it was Men's Wearhouse as a greeter, not real warehouse work
jokes.explanation: Perfect Michael moment. The misdirection from 'warehouse' to 'Men's Wearhouse' and then further deflation to 'greeter' shows his desperate attempts to seem tough.

jokes.text: Hello, I'm Michael. Welcome to Men's Wearhouse. We have a special on khaki pants today.
jokes.setup: Michael demonstrating his greeter experience
jokes.punchline: The overly cheerful, generic retail greeting
jokes.explanation: Good character follow-through but the retail voice impression is fairly standard. Works as continuation of the warehouse misdirection.

jokes.text: 51. / Oh, don't be that person. / Come on, that is lame. / It's a strategy. / It's called being smart.
jokes.setup: Jelly bean counting contest, Oscar bids one more than previous guess
jokes.punchline: Oscar defending his annoying 'Price is Right' strategy as smart
jokes.explanation: Relatable office humor about that annoying person in guessing games. Oscar's smug justification is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: He has spent hours up here at reception with you. Hours and hours. / Okay... okay. / No, constantly... like, for years.
jokes.setup: Angela accusing Jim of having unfair advantage in jelly bean count
jokes.punchline: Her escalating description of time spent at reception becomes uncomfortably specific
jokes.explanation: Angela's jealousy manifesting as detailed surveillance of Jim and Pam's interactions. Her escalation from 'hours' to 'constantly... for years' reveals too much.

jokes.text: Nobody commits suicide because they work with a bailer.
jokes.setup: Michael comparing depression to bailer safety
jokes.punchline: His completely wrong logic about suicide causes
jokes.explanation: Dark humor with Michael's complete misunderstanding of mental health vs. workplace safety. Perfectly wrong in a way that's both funny and troubling.

jokes.text: You go to the science museum and you put your hand on a metal ball. Your hair sticks up straight. And you know science.
jokes.setup: Michael explaining the need for visual aids in safety training
jokes.punchline: His childlike understanding that touching a static ball means 'you know science'
jokes.explanation: Excellent Michael logic. His reductive understanding that one interactive exhibit equals understanding all of science is perfectly childish and quotable.

jokes.text: Because you know what our killer is? Depression. Wolves. Depression.
jokes.setup: Michael listing office dangers
jokes.punchline: Randomly includes 'wolves' between two mentions of depression
jokes.explanation: Absurdist perfection. The random 'wolves' insertion between two serious mentions of depression is unexpected and memorable. Economy of language with maximum impact.

jokes.text: And I jump. And they freak out. And they get to see the dangers of depression with their own eyes. Nice side note... they might think, hey, I should've been nicer to Michael. But that's not why I'm doing this.
jokes.setup: Michael explaining his fake suicide plan
jokes.punchline: Claims his plan isn't for sympathy while clearly describing wanting sympathy
jokes.explanation: Perfect Michael self-deception. He describes exactly what he wants (sympathy) while claiming that's not his motivation. The 'nice side note' reveals everything.

jokes.text: Ta-freakin'-da!
jokes.setup: Michael describing his dramatic reveal after the fake jump
jokes.punchline: His enthusiastic, childish version of 'ta-da'
jokes.explanation: Cute Michael-ism. The unnecessary profanity inserted into 'ta-da' shows his attempt to make everything more dramatic and adult.

jokes.text: Ryan describing Netflix queue management in elaborate detail while others time him
jokes.setup: Ryan launches into detailed explanation of Netflix
jokes.punchline: Others are timing him and betting on his verbal tics, treating him like entertainment
jokes.explanation: Brilliant cringe comedy. Ryan's over-explanation of something simple while being unknowingly studied and bet upon creates multiple layers of comedy and discomfort.

jokes.text: You're such a ditz.
jokes.setup: Ryan admits he 'forgot' how Netflix works after his elaborate explanation
jokes.punchline: Kelly calling him a ditz for not knowing something he just spent 3 minutes explaining
jokes.explanation: Perfect Kelly response. She completely misses that Ryan just demonstrated extensive Netflix knowledge, calling him dumb for the exact opposite reason.

jokes.text: Maybe we should test this first, Letterman-style. Throw a TV over. / We measured it once. / Go buy some watermelons. / Seedless?
jokes.setup: Discussion of testing the roof jump with objects
jokes.punchline: Dwight asking if the watermelons should be seedless for the crash test
jokes.explanation: Dwight's literal-mindedness asking about seeded vs. seedless watermelons for a crash test shows his complete misunderstanding of what matters in this situation.

jokes.text: I found a potato.
jokes.setup: Creed is eating what appears to be an apple
jokes.punchline: He announces he found a potato, suggesting confusion about what he's eating
jokes.explanation: Pure Creed. Maximum character integration with minimal words. His confusion about basic foods while casually eating is perfectly mysterious and unsettling.

jokes.text: Car alarm and crash aftermath response
jokes.setup: Watermelon test goes wrong, hits car
jokes.punchline: Michael's panicked, specific instructions about cleanup and legal concerns
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy payoff with Michael's immediate panic management. The specificity of calling a lawyer shows his awareness that this is very wrong.

jokes.text: If it's Stanley's, call the offices of James P. Albini. See if he handles hate crimes.
jokes.setup: Michael giving instructions about the damaged car
jokes.punchline: Implies Stanley might consider the accident a hate crime
jokes.explanation: Dark humor showing Michael's awareness of his problematic relationship with Stanley. The specific lawyer name adds believability to his panic.

jokes.text: Have Patch do it... or the sea monster.
jokes.setup: Michael needs someone to dispose of the trampoline in the bailer
jokes.punchline: References the warehouse workers by their nicknames from earlier
jokes.explanation: Nice callback to the 'sea monster' insult from earlier, showing Michael absorbed the warehouse dynamic even during chaos.

jokes.text: I'm temporarily lifting the shun. / Thank you. / It means nothing.
jokes.setup: Dwight needs Andy's help so lifts the shunning
jokes.punchline: Immediately clarifies that lifting the shun means nothing
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dwight logic. He maintains the philosophical purity of shunning while pragmatically lifting it for business needs.

jokes.text: You mean a moon bounce? / What do you think? You've got an hour. / I'm gonna need petty cash. / Shunning resumed.
jokes.setup: Dwight asking Andy to acquire inflatable castle
jokes.punchline: Immediate resumption of shunning after getting agreement
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation of the shunning bit. Dwight's businesslike use of Andy followed by immediate return to shunning shows perfect character consistency.

jokes.text: Do you... do you want a drawbridge? / Un-shun. / Yeah, that sounds good. Re-shun.
jokes.setup: Andy asking about castle features while being shunned
jokes.punchline: Dwight's rapid un-shun/re-shun cycle for the single response
jokes.explanation: Peak shunning comedy. The rapid-fire un-shun/re-shun for a single business response is perfectly absurd and shows Dwight's commitment to both the task and the punishment.

jokes.text: When you land, try and land like an eight-year-old. These bouncy castles are not designed for adults.
jokes.setup: Dwight giving Michael safety advice for the bounce castle
jokes.punchline: Specific instruction to 'land like an eight-year-old'
jokes.explanation: Great Dwight moment combining practical safety concern with absurd specificity. 'Land like an eight-year-old' is memorable and oddly logical.

jokes.text: If we keep doing them, I'm not gonna want to jump. / This is about doing, not thinking.
jokes.setup: Michael worried that testing will make him not want to do the stunt
jokes.punchline: Dwight's motivational advice to stop thinking
jokes.explanation: Good character dynamic. Michael's fear is rational but Dwight encourages the dangerous behavior with pseudo-motivational speak.

jokes.text: Is it nice outside? / It's gorgeous. Let's go! / Do I need my jacket? / No, really, it's very nice. / Will I be too warm in a long sleeve T? / Everyone's gonna be fine in exactly what they're wearing! Let's go!
jokes.setup: Andy trying to get everyone outside urgently
jokes.punchline: Increasingly frustrated responses to mundane clothing questions during emergency
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation comedy. The contrast between Michael's life-or-death situation and office workers' clothing concerns, with Andy's growing desperation, is beautifully executed.

jokes.text: Depression? Isn't that just a fancy word for feeling bummed out? / Dwight, you ignorant slut.
jokes.setup: Dwight minimizing depression during fake suicide
jokes.punchline: Michael's 'ignorant slut' insult (Saturday Night Live reference)
jokes.explanation: Classic callback to SNL's 'Jane, you ignorant slut.' While not original, Michael's delivery and the context of fake suicide makes it effective and highly quotable.

jokes.text: If someone gives you 10,000 to 1 on anything, you take it. If John Mellencamp ever wins an Oscar, I am going to be a very rich dude.
jokes.setup: Kevin explaining betting philosophy about Michael's suicide being fake
jokes.punchline: Applying same logic to impossible John Mellencamp Oscar bet
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Kevin moment. The logic is sound but the example of John Mellencamp winning an Oscar is perfectly absurd and specific. Shows his gambling mindset applied to ridiculous scenarios.

jokes.text: Well, you know, the first performance was a little off. But I really think they hit their stride in the second show. / Um... / Might even bring my parents tomorrow to the matinee.
jokes.setup: Michael repeating his fake suicide performance
jokes.punchline: Oscar reviewing it like a theater critic, considering bringing family
jokes.explanation: Exceptional meta-comedy. Oscar treating Michael's fake suicide as a theatrical performance worthy of professional review and family attendance is brilliantly absurd and perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Hey, check it out. There's a castle over there. / Oh, my God. There is a castle. / No, there's nothing to see over there, people. There's nothing to see.
jokes.setup: Michael's performance being upstaged by discovery of bounce castle
jokes.punchline: Michael desperately trying to redirect attention from his own prop
jokes.explanation: Perfect dramatic irony. The audience knows the castle is Michael's safety net, so his desperate attempts to hide it while maintaining the suicide act creates excellent tension and comedy.

jokes.text: He's going to kill himself pretending to kill himself.
jokes.setup: Michael about to jump onto the poorly positioned bouncy castle
jokes.punchline: Jim's perfect summary of the absurd ironic situation
jokes.explanation: Perfect comedic observation with maximum economy. Jim captures the entire absurd situation in seven words that are both accurate and hilarious. The irony is perfectly crystallized.

jokes.text: It's, uh... A Repliee Q1 Expo female robot. They're only available in Japan. / Dwight, you are such a liar.
jokes.setup: Dwight trying to help Pam's lie about having a present for Michael
jokes.punchline: His oddly specific and completely inappropriate 'present' suggestion
jokes.explanation: Pure Dwight absurdity. The specific model number and Japan detail shows his weird expertise, while offering a sex robot as suicide prevention is perfectly inappropriate and character-driven.

jokes.text: You told me that I lead a... a cushy, wimpy, NERF life. / Yeah, but I never said you had nothing to live for.
jokes.setup: Michael referencing Darryl's earlier insults as justification for jumping
jokes.punchline: Darryl's clarification that calling him wimpy wasn't a death sentence
jokes.explanation: Good callback structure. Darryl's reasonable response to Michael's dramatic overinterpretation shows the disconnect between workplace teasing and actual death wishes.

jokes.text: I mean, it takes courage just to be you. To get out of bed every single day knowing full well you gotta be you. / Do you really mean that? / I couldn't do it. I ain't that strong and I ain't that brave.
jokes.setup: Darryl seemingly giving Michael encouraging words about being brave
jokes.punchline: Reveals he means being Michael is so terrible it requires courage to live with yourself
jokes.explanation: Masterpiece of dark comedy. Darryl's 'compliment' is actually a devastating insult disguised as motivation. The structure is perfect - it sounds supportive until the brutal punchline lands.

jokes.text: You Braveheart, man. / I Braveheart. I am.
jokes.setup: Darryl continuing the backhanded compliment using Braveheart reference
jokes.punchline: Michael accepting it as genuine praise and adopting the grammar
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up to the courage bit. Michael's complete misunderstanding and adoption of the grammar ('I Braveheart') shows his desperate need for validation.

jokes.text: Am I a hero? I really can't say. But, yes.
jokes.setup: Michael's talking head reflecting on whether he's a hero
jokes.punchline: Claims he can't say, then immediately says yes
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael ending. The false modesty immediately contradicted by self-aggrandizement is perfect character work. The structure and timing are flawless.

tmdb_overview: Andy returns from anger management; Dwight decides to shun him for three years.
